63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
98	  \[
99	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
100	      \longrightarrow 0
101	  \]
102	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
103	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
104	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
105	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
106	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
107	  \[
108	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
109	  \]
110	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
111	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
112	  \[
113	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
114	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
115	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
116	  \]
117	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
118	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
119	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
120	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
124	
125	\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
126	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
127	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
128	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
129	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
130	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
131	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
132	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
133	  % L5594--5617).
134	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
135	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
136	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
137	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
138	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
139	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
140	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
141	  % between abelian categories and assume
142	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
143	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
144	  % \begin{enumerate}
145	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
146	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
147	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
148	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
149	  % \end{enumerate}"
150	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
151	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
152	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
153	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
154	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
155	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
156	  \[
157	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
158	  \]
159	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
160	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
161	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
162	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
163	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
164	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
165	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
166	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
167	
168	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
169	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
170	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
171	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
172	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
176	
177	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
178	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
179	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
180	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
181	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
182	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
183	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
184	lift.
185	
186	\begin{lemma}[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
187	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
188	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
189	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
190	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
191	  \[
192	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
193	  \]
194	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
195	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
196	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
197	  \[
198	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
199	  \]
200	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
201	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
202	  \[
203	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
204	      \longrightarrow 0
205	  \]
206	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
207	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
208	\end{lemma}
209	
210	\begin{proof}
211	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
212	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
213	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
214	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
215	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
216	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
217	  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
218	  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
219	  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
220	  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
221	  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
222	  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
223	  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
224	  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
225	  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
226	  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
227	  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
228	  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
229	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
230	  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
231	  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
232	  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
233	\end{proof}
234	
235	\section{The dimension-shift step}
236	
237	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
238	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
239	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
240	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
241	complex-level homology long exact sequence
242	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
243	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
244	sub-lemma below.
245	
246	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
247	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
248	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
249	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
250	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
251	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
252	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
253	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
254	  % between abelian categories and assume
255	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
256	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
257	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
258	  % \begin{enumerate}
259	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
260	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
261	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
262	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
263	  % \end{enumerate}
264	  % In each of the three cases
265	  % $$
266	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
267	  % $$
268	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
269	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
270	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
271	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
272	  \[
273	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
274	  \]
275	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
276	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
277	  \begin{enumerate}
278	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
279	      furnish isomorphisms
280	      \[
281	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
282	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
283	      \]
284	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
285	      \[
286	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
287	      \]
288	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
289	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
290	  \end{enumerate}
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
294	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
295	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
296	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
297	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
298	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
299	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
300	\begin{proof}
301	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
302	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
303	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
304	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
305	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
306	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
307	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
308	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
309	
310	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
311	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
312	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
313	  complexes
314	  \[
315	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
316	  \]
317	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
318	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
319	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
320	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
321	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
322	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
323	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
324	  \[
325	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
326	      \longrightarrow 0
327	  \]
328	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
329	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
330	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
331	  \[
332	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
333	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
334	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
335	  \]
336	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
337	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
338	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
339	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
340	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
341	  sequence of right-derived functors
342	  \[
343	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
344	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
345	  \]
346	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
347	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
348	  \[
349	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
350	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
351	  \]
352	
353	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
354	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
355	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
356	  \[
357	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
358	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
359	  \]
360	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
361	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
362	  In the lowest degree the segment
363	  \[
364	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
365	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
366	  \]
367	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
368	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
369	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
370	  part (2).
371	\end{proof}

373	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
374	
375	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
376	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
377	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
378	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
379	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
380	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
381	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
382	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
383	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
384	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
385	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
386	  % L5785--5811).
387	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
388	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
389	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
390	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
391	  % $$
392	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
393	  % $$
394	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
395	  % $RF$."
396	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
397	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
398	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
399	  % and we obtain an exact functor
400	  % $$
401	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
402	  % $$
403	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
404	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
405	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
406	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
407	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
408	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
409	  Let
410	  \[
411	    0 \longrightarrow A
412	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
413	      \longrightarrow \cdots
414	  \]
415	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
416	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
417	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
418	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
419	  \[
420	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
421	  \]
422	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
423	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
424	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
425	  object.
426	\end{theorem}
427	
428	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
429	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
430	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
431	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
432	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
433	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
434	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
435	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
436	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
437	% the long exact cohomology sequence
438	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
439	% to an exact sequence
440	% $$
441	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
442	% $$
443	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
444	\begin{proof}
445	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
446	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
447	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
448	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
449	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
450	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
451	  resolution into the
452	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
453	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
454	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
455	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
456	  \[
457	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
458	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
459	  \]
460	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
461	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
462	
463	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
464	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
465	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
466	  \[
467	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
468	      = (R^0 G)(A),
469	  \]
470	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
471	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
472	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
473	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
474	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
475	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
476	  \[
477	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
478	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
479	  \]
480	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
481	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
482	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
483	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
484	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
485	
486	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
487	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
488	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
489	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
490	  \[
491	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
492	  \]
493	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
494	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
495	  isomorphisms down the staircase
496	  \[
497	    (R^n G)(A)
498	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
499	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
500	      \;\cong\; \cdots
501	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
502	  \]
503	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
504	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
505	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
506	  \[
507	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
508	  \]
509	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
510	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
511	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
512	  \[
513	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
514	      \;\cong\;
515	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
516	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
517	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
518	  \]
519	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
520	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
521	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
522	
523	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
524	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
525	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
526	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
527	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
528	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
529	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
530	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
531	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
532	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
533	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
534	\end{proof}
